nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in shallow subtidal environments — rocky bottoms, reef rubble, oyster beds, sponges, and rock crevices — at depths of roughly 2–5 m. Tends to be cryptic and largely nocturnal in the wild.

diet.notes: Generally considered an opportunistic scavenger and meaty-food carnivore. Has been documented accepting mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped marine fish or shellfish flesh, sinking pellets, and quality frozen carnivore mixes. In an established reef tank it tends to pick at detritus, uneaten food, and decomposing material between feedings. Famous in the trade for consuming Aiptasia anemones, though individual results vary — well-fed shrimp may ignore Aiptasia altogether, and reliable Aiptasia control is most consistently reported for Lysmata boggessi rather than true L. wurdemanni.

avoidWith: predatory fish (lionfish, large wrasses, large dottybacks, hawkfish, triggerfish)
ornamental anemones (Entacmaea, Heteractis) — shrimp may be stung or eaten
any copper-based medication in the system

summary: A small, translucent Western Atlantic cleaner-scavenger shrimp (Lysmata wurdemanni complex) striped with thin red longitudinal bands. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, and famously sold as a biological control for Aiptasia pest anemones.
careNotes:
The Peppermint Shrimp is a small, peaceful, largely nocturnal shrimp from the tropical Western Atlantic — the US east coast, Gulf of Mexico, Florida Keys, and Bahamas. In the aquarium it tends to spend daylight hours tucked into caves, crevices, and shaded overhangs in the rockwork, emerging at feeding time and after lights-out to pick over the substrate for detritus, uneaten food, and small meaty items. A mature reef aquascape with ample hiding places and at least one open scavenging zone is generally advised.

Like all decapod crustaceans, Peppermint Shrimp are highly sensitive to copper-based medications and to swings in salinity, pH, or temperature. Drip-acclimation over 1–2 hours is generally advised on introduction. Iodine supplementation is sometimes recommended to support clean molts, though stable reef-tank parameters and good nutrition are typically sufficient; freshly molted shrimp are vulnerable and tend to hide for a day or two until the new exoskeleton hardens.

Generally considered reef-safe, with caveats: have been known to occasionally nip at large-polyp stony corals, zoanthids, or stressed/sessile invertebrates, especially if underfed. Compatible with most peaceful reef fish but should not be housed with predatory species that target shrimp (lionfish, large wrasses, large dottybacks, hawkfish, triggerfish, moray eels). Tends to be sociable with conspecifics and is commonly kept in small groups of 2–5; numbers should be matched to tank size and feeding regime, with extra shrimp often added when Aiptasia control is the goal.

Taxonomic note — species complex: 'Peppermint Shrimp' in the aquarium trade is not a single species. Rhyne & Lin's 2006 taxonomic revision split the historic 'Lysmata wurdemanni' into a complex of cryptic species, including at least L. wurdemanni, L. boggessi, L. ankeri, L. rathbunae, L. pederseni, and L. bahia, with recent molecular work suggesting up to 9 species exist across the range. Reliable Aiptasia predation is most consistently associated with Lysmata boggessi, the Florida/Gulf species typically supplied to North American retailers (including LiveAquaria, which explicitly notes most of its 'Peppermint Shrimp' are L. boggessi from the Florida Keys or Gulf of Mexico). True L. wurdemanni has a more limited range (Atlantic US to Texas) and only rarely eats Aiptasia. Hobbyists buying for Aiptasia control should ideally request L. boggessi by name; unfortunately most retailers sell to the trade name and do not distinguish.

Cross-source notes: family placement varies — older retailer pages list Hippolytidae, while modern taxonomy (Wikipedia, World Register of Marine Species) places the genus in Lysmatidae; Lysmatidae is used here. Adult size figures span 5 cm (LiveAquaria, AlgaeBarn '~2 in') to 7 cm (Wikipedia, citing maximum 2.8 in); the 3.5–5 cm range here reflects the typical adult-in-aquarium reality. Temperature recommendations range from 22.2–25.6°C (LiveAquaria) to 24–27°C (hobby guides); the averaged 22–26°C is used. Tank-size guidance is universally light (most sources state '10 gal / ~38 L minimum') because the animal itself is small; 40 L is the conservative nice-number floor.
breedingNotes: Lysmata species are protandric simultaneous hermaphrodites — individuals mature first as males, then begin functioning as both sexes simultaneously, so any two adults can pair and produce eggs. Hatching of zoea larvae has been reported routinely in the aquarium, but larval rearing requires a dedicated planktonic system (gentle flow, no mechanical filtration, live rotifer/Artemia nauplii feeds) and is considered challenging at the hobby scale. Lysmata boggessi is now commercially captive-bred (e.g., Biota, AlgaeBarn) and captive-bred specimens are generally hardier and better adapted to aquarium life than wild-caught.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level, reef-safe status, diet (scavenger plus meaty foods), copper sensitivity, and the species-complex note that retailer stock is most often L. boggessi from Florida/Gulf. crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts roughly every 3–6 weeks under stable reef conditions; younger and faster-growing shrimp molt more frequently. The freshly molted shrimp is soft and vulnerable for about 24–48 hours and tends to hide; leave the shed exuvia in the tank for the shrimp to consume (recovers calcium and trace elements). Stable salinity and trace-element supplementation (iodine in particular) are commonly cited as supporting clean molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Egg-carrying females are common in the aquarium; rearing the planktonic zoea larvae is the bottleneck and typically requires a dedicated larval tank and live feeds.
